title: Gustavo Jesus Borjas
text: My name is Gustavo Jesus Borjas. I am a 3rd year student in the Case Western Reserve University Medical Science Training Program (CWRU MSTP) working towards a dual MD/PhD degree. I am part of the Letterio Lab in the pathology department of CWRU.

title: Gustavo Jesus Borjas
text:
Research
My interests lies in developing novel therapeutic strategies to modulate the immune system for the treatment of cancer and autoimmune diseases. Read more about my research here!

title: CV
text: All you would want to know about my professional life.

title: Gustavo Jesus Borjas
text:
A Little about me
Originally born in Venezuela, I grew up in warm and sunny Orlando, FL. I went on to attend Emory University where I found a deeply rooted interest in medical research, leading me to pursue a MD/PhD dual degree program. I aim to become a physician-scientist working to translate bench research into the clinical space.
In between college and medical school, I spent time in two research labs further ignited and reaffirmed my career goal in medical research. First, I worked under the supervision of Drs. Roger Li and Jose Conejo-Garcia at Moffitt cancer center. There, my main projects were working on the immune correlates of a Phase Ib clinical trial of a novel form of immunotherapy for muscle invasive bladder cancer (Li, 2025). After Moffitt, I spent a second year at UChicago under the supervision of Drs. Jeffrey Hubbell and Lisa Volpatti working on developing novel immunotherapies for cancer and autoimmune diseases.
Moving forward, I came under the mentorship of Dr. John Letterio. Letterio has long worked on different modalities of drug development throughout his career, including research into immunomodulatory approaches for cancer and autoimmune diseases. Naturally, I’ve fit in well into his research programs and I continue to thrive under his mentorship and in the wider CWRU community.

title: Gustavo Jesus Borjas
text:
About me
Originally born in Venezuela, I grew up in warm and sunny Orlando, FL. I went on to attend Emory University where I found a deeply rooted interest in medical research, leading me to pursue a MD/PhD dual degree program. I aim to become a physician-scientist working to translate bench research into the clinical space.
In between college and medical school, I spent time in two research labs further ignited and reaffirmed my career goal in medical research. First, I worked under the supervision of Drs. Roger Li and Jose Conejo-Garcia at Moffitt cancer center. There, my main projects were working on the immune correlates of a Phase Ib clinical trial of a novel form of immunotherapy for muscle invasive bladder cancer (Li, 2025). After Moffitt, I spent a second year at the University of Chicago under the supervision of Drs. Jeffrey Hubbell and Lisa Volpatti working on developing novel immunotherapies for cancer and autoimmune diseases.
Moving forward, I came under the mentorship of Dr. John Letterio. Letterio has long worked on different modalities of drug development throughout his career, including research into developing novel immune-based therapeutic strategies. Naturally, I have fit in well into his research programs and I continue to thrive under his mentorship and in the wider CWRU community.

title: Research
text:
Nrf2 is a master transcriptional regulator of metabolism, the immune response, and of cytoprotective proteins. In the basal state, Nrf2 is bound and tethered to the perinuclear space by Keap1 which also mediates degradation of Nrf2 by the ubiquitin-proteasome system. Thus, Keap1 negatively regulates Nrf2 by regulating both degradation rate and localization. In response to oxidative stress, such as inflammation, Keap1:Nrf2 dissociate, and Nrf2 translocates to bind Antioxidant Response Element (ARE) motifs in the genome, leading to activation of Nrf2-associated genes such as those associated with cellular respiration and anti-inflammatory cytokine signaling. Small molecules that disrupt the Keap1:Nrf2 interaction have advanced to the clinic, including the triterpenoid Skyclarys® (Friedreich’s ataxia) and dimethyl fumarate (Tecfidera®/Fumaderm® for MS/psoriasis), while others are being studied for various inflammatory diseases. However, despite their therapeutic potential, Nrf2 activators have had limited clinical translation due to off-target effects.
My current project lies in developing a novel targeting strategy to specifically activate Nrf2 in immune cells to avoid the off-target toxicities associated with current small molecule inhibitors.



The Keap1-Nrf2 Pathway (Suzuki, Current Opinion in Toxicology 2016)

title: Research
text:
Nrf2 is a master transcriptional regulator of metabolism, the immune response, and of cytoprotective proteins. In the basal state, Nrf2 is bound and tethered to the perinuclear space by Keap1 which also mediates degradation of Nrf2 by the ubiquitin-proteasome system. Thus, Keap1 negatively regulates Nrf2 by regulating both degradation rate and localization. In response to oxidative stress, such as inflammation, Keap1:Nrf2 dissociate, and Nrf2 translocates to bind Antioxidant Response Element (ARE) motifs in the genome, leading to activation of Nrf2-associated genes such as those associated with cellular respiration and anti-inflammatory cytokine signaling. Small molecules that disrupt the Keap1:Nrf2 interaction have advanced to the clinic, including the triterpenoid Skyclarys® (Friedreich’s ataxia) and dimethyl fumarate (Tecfidera®/Fumaderm® for MS/psoriasis), while others are being studied for various inflammatory diseases. However, despite their therapeutic potential, Nrf2 activators have had limited clinical translation due to off-target effects.
My current project lies in developing a novel targeting strategy to specifically activate Nrf2 in immune cells to avoid the off-target toxicities associated with current small molecule inhibitors.
